text: The surgeon navigates the right pelvic side wall, identifying a clip near the iliorectal anastomosis. Dense adhesions are visible, with the external iliac artery being drawn onto the rectum. The surgeon attempts to create a plane by sweeping the adhesions away, despite the absence of a normal tissue plane.

The surgeon is performing an abdominal perineal resection, focusing on the right lateral aspect of the rectum. They are carefully dissecting to identify the retro rectal plane, aiming to reach the space over the sacral promontory. The right ureter is visible, being gently swept down from the rectum, providing reassurance in the absence of ureteral catheters. The surgeon continues to work anteriorly, attempting to identify the anastomosis.

The surgeon navigates the pelvic region, working between the bladder and bowel, attempting to identify the rectum amidst significant adhesions and fibrosis. The dissection progresses around the left anterior side, focusing on the bowel edge and a fused plane likely due to previous surgery. The surgeon returns to the right side, working higher over the sacral promontory, using the right ureter as a lateral border. The rectal mesentery is identified, and the surgeon follows the TME plane, noting the scarred tissue and visualizing the left common iliac vein and the posterior window of the TME plane near the bladder.

The surgeon works down the left side of the pelvis, attempting to separate tissue from the retroperitoneum. The iliac vessels are visible on the left side of the screen. A large wad of fat is observed between the bowel and the iliac, with no clear planes identified. The anterior surface of the rectum is visible, and the surgeon continues dissection along the right TME plane, with the ureter being pulled up. The mesorectal fascia and walled iris fascia are identified posterior to the sacral promontory, and the hypogastric plexus nerves are visible on the right side.

The surgeon develops a normal appearing plane posteriorly along the sacrum, aiming to extend this plane laterally on both sides to facilitate easier lateral dissection. The ureter is visible, and the surgeon carefully navigates the right-hand side, leaving some mesorectum on the right side wall to avoid proximity to the ureter. The dissection progresses anteriorly beneath the prostate and vesicles, encountering a fused plane.

The surgeon attempts lateral dissection on the left side of the rectum, encountering a large wad of fat. They explore a plane between the bowel wall and the fat, aiming to connect with a previously dissected medial plane. The left common iliac vein and artery are identified, but the ureter remains elusive. A sponge is placed over the vessels to aid in dissection. The surgeon clamps proximal to the suspected anastomosis, noting the rectum filled with polyps. Clips are placed where polyps stop, and the small bowel is exteriorized through a fan and steel incision. The surgeon finds a safer window into the retroperitoneum, identifying the ureter lateral to the iliac, ensuring safe dissection. The thick, fibrous tissue requires multiple burns and cuts with a ligature to progress.

The surgeon completes the dissection from the abdominal side, ensuring better exposure for the perineal dissection. The procedure is performed in a lithotomy position, with a partner handling residual lysis of adhesions through a fan and steel incision. The ileum is disconnected at the clip level and exteriorized through the incision. Surgical gauze is placed from the perineal side, and the right ureter is visible, skeletonized but not problematic. The end of the ileum is transferred to the right lower quadrant port site for ileostomy, ensuring no twisting of the mesentery. The mesentery is oriented towards the head, and the bowel towards the feet.
